4. What is a Symbol, why was it introduced, and how does it prevent property name collisions?

12. What advanced JavaScript concepts are most important for senior frontend developers to understand?
1. What is an Array, how do you create one, and why does typeof [] return "object" (Arrays vs Objects)?
2. What is the difference between Mutable and Immutable Array methods, and why are immutable methods preferred in React?
3. What do push(), pop(), shift(), unshift(), slice(), and splice() do, and how do slice()/splice() differ?
4. What is the difference between map(), forEach(), filter(), find(), and reduce() — and when do you use each?
5. What is the difference between some() and every(), and between includes() and indexOf()?
6. What do sort() and toSorted() do, and why doesn't sort() sort numbers correctly by default?
7. What is the difference between flat() and flatMap(), and how would you flatten a nested array?
8. What is an Array-like Object, and how do you convert one into a real Array (Array.from() vs spread)?

12. How would you sort an array of objects, and why should you avoid mutating arrays in React state?
13. What is the difference between Array.isArray(), Array.of(), and Array.from()?
1. What is synchronous vs asynchronous programming, and why does JavaScript need it?
2. What is a Callback Function, what is Callback Hell, and how do Promises solve it?
3. What is a Promise, what are its three states (Pending/Fulfilled/Rejected), and how do you create one?

11. What is an unhandled Promise rejection, and how can it affect an application?
12. How would you fetch data from multiple APIs simultaneously, retry a failed request, or handle partial API failures?
13. How do modern frameworks like React and Next.js rely on Promises?
1. What is the DOM, how does the browser construct it, and what's the difference between getElementById(), querySelector(), and querySelectorAll()?
2. What is the difference between innerHTML, innerText, and textContent, and how do you create/append/remove DOM elements?
3. What is DOM Traversal (parentNode/parentElement, children/childNodes), and what is a Document Fragment used for?

7. What is Event Delegation, why is it useful, and how does it improve performance for dynamically added elements?

Commands used constantly while working with Git — initial setup, branching, undoing mistakes, and the stuff I'd otherwise have to re-Google every time. Copy-paste first, explanation after.

Set your global name and email (shows up on every commit) git config --global user.name "Your Name" git config --global user.email "you@example.com" Set the default branch name for new repos git config --global init.defaultBranch main Set your default editor (used for commit messages, rebase, etc.) git config --global core.editor "code --wait" Check your current config git config --list Generate an SSH key (so you don't have to type a password every push) ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "you@example.com" eval "$(ssh-agent -s)" ssh-add ~/.ssh/id_ed25519 Copy your public key (to paste into GitHub → Settings → SSH and GPG keys) cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub Test the SSH connection ssh -T git@github.com

Initialize a new repo git init Clone a repo git clone git@github.com:username/repo.git Check status (what's staged, unstaged, untracked) git status Stage changes git add file.txt git add . # stage everything Commit staged changes git commit -m "message" Edit the last commit (message or forgotten files, before pushing) git commit --amend See unstaged changes git diff See staged changes git diff --staged View commit history git log --oneline --graph --all

List branches git branch Create a branch git branch feature-name Switch to a branch git switch feature-name Create and switch in one step git switch -c feature-name Rename a branch git branch -m old-name new-name Delete a local branch git branch -d feature-name # safe — refuses if unmerged git branch -D feature-name # force delete Delete a remote branch git push origin --delete feature-name

Merge a branch into your current branch git merge feature-name Rebase your current branch onto another git rebase main Interactive rebase (squash, reorder, or edit the last N commits) git rebase -i HEAD~3 Continue after resolving a conflict git rebase --continue # if rebasing git merge --continue # if merging Abort and back out entirely git rebase --abort git merge --abort Merge vs rebase, quickly: merge keeps full history and adds a merge commit — safe for shared branches. Rebase rewrites your branch's commits on top of another, giving a cleaner linear history — fine for your own unpushed branches, risky on anything others have already pulled.

View configured remotes git remote -v Add a remote git remote add origin git@github.com:username/repo.git Fetch changes without merging git fetch Pull (fetch + merge) git pull Pull with rebase instead of merge (avoids extra merge commits) git pull --rebase Push git push Push a new branch and set upstream tracking git push -u origin feature-name Force-push safely (fails if someone else pushed in the meantime, unlike plain --force) git push --force-with-lease

Unstage a file (keep the changes) git restore --staged file.txt Discard local changes to a file (changes are gone — careful) git restore file.txt Undo the last commit, keep changes staged git reset --soft HEAD~1 Undo the last commit, keep changes unstaged git reset HEAD~1 Undo the last commit and discard the changes entirely (careful — no undo) git reset --hard HEAD~1 Revert a commit safely (creates a new commit that undoes it — fine for shared/pushed history) git revert <commit-hash> Recover something you thought you lost git reflog git reset --hard <hash-from-reflog>

Stash current changes git stash Stash including untracked files git stash -u List stashes git stash list Reapply the latest stash, keep it in the list git stash apply Reapply the latest stash and remove it git stash pop Delete a stash git stash drop

Readable log with branches git log --oneline --graph --decorate --all See who last changed each line of a file git blame file.txt Show a specific commit's full diff git show <commit-hash> Search commit messages git log --grep="keyword" See a file's full change history git log -p -- file.txt

List tags git tag Create a lightweight tag git tag v1.0.0 Create an annotated tag (with a message — preferred for releases) git tag -a v1.0.0 -m "Release 1.0.0" Push a tag git push origin v1.0.0 Push all tags git push origin --tags Delete a tag git tag -d v1.0.0

Detached HEAD — you're not on any branch, just a specific commit. If you've made commits here you want to keep, run git switch -c new-branch right away to save them onto a real branch before switching away. Committed to the wrong branch (not pushed yet) → git branch correct-branch to save a branch at the current point, then git reset --hard HEAD~1 on the wrong branch to remove the commit, then git switch correct-branch. Accidentally pushed a secret → rotate/revoke the secret immediately — pushing a fix doesn't remove it from history. Removing it fully needs git filter-repo or the BFG Repo-Cleaner, followed by a force-push. Merge conflict panic → open the conflicted file, find the <<<<<<< / ======= / >>>>>>> markers, manually decide what stays, delete the markers, then git add <file> and finish with git commit (merge) or git rebase --continue (rebase). Forgot to .gitignore something already tracked (like node_modules) → add it to .gitignore, then git rm -r --cached node_modules and commit. Repo feels bloated → git count-objects -vH shows total size; git rev-list --objects --all | git cat-file --batch-check='%(objecttype) %(objectname) %(objectsize) %(rest)' | sort -k3 -n -r | head finds the largest files in history.

Personal developer notes and quick-reference commands — Git, Docker, Linux, CI/CD, and more, growing as I learn. This isn't a tutorial series. It's a quick-reference vault: copy-paste commands, working config templates, and troubleshooting notes for the tools I actually use day-to-day.
Git — initial setup, branching, undoing mistakes, and the commands I'd otherwise re-Google every time More sections (Docker, Linux, CI/CD, MongoDB) will be added as notes get written.

Learning a tool once isn't the same as remembering it under pressure. This site exists to close that gap — every note here is written from a real moment where I got stuck, searched for the fix, and decided to never search for it again.

Question banks for technical interviews — JavaScript, TypeScript, React, Next.js, and more, growing as I study. This isn't a tutorial series. It's a self-test vault: each topic is trimmed down to the questions that actually get asked, one collapsible card per question so I can read it, try to answer it out loud, then reveal the answer to check myself.

JavaScript — fundamentals, execution context, scope, closures, this, async, the event loop, and more, split topic by topic More subjects (TypeScript, React, Next.js) will be added as notes get written.

Knowing an answer and being able to say it clearly under pressure are two different skills. Every question here is picked because it's genuinely likely to come up — not padded out for volume — so a full pass through a topic stays realistic to actually do before an interview, not just something to skim once and forget.
